one_line_takeaway: A systematic attack-and-defense framework showing that hand-crafted CoT/sandwich/few-shot prompts extract system prompts verbatim from five SOTA LLMs at up to ~99% ASR, and that only substring-based output filtering, not instruction-based defenses, reliably stops them.
key_finding: SOTA LLMs are highly vulnerable to simple prompting-based system prompt extraction: Llama-3 CoT reaches ~99% ASR on short prompts and 92-93% on long prompts; GPT-4 extended sandwich reaches ~99% ASR on the ChatGPT Roles dataset and 87% on long prompts; even GPT-4.1 hits 80% ASR on long prompts. The paper's CoT attack beats all cited baselines in average EM (Llama 0.874 vs PLeak 0.728; Falcon 0.715 vs PLeak 0.595). On defenses, the proposed system prompt filtering is by far the most effective, collapsing ASR (e.g., Llama-3 CoT from 99% down to 0.16%), while the two instruction-based defenses (instruction defense, sandwich defense) are inconsistent: strong on Llama-3/GPT-4/GPT-4.1 but weak on Gemma-2, where ASR remains high (up to ~68%). Short system prompts are consistently more vulnerable than long ones.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SPE-LLM, a framework to systematically evaluate system prompt extraction attacks AND defenses in LLMs. Its own three contributions are: (1) a set of hand-designed adversarial queries (CoT prompting, Few-shot prompting, and an 'extended sandwich' attack that adapts the multilingual sandwich-attack jailbreak into a same-language single-layer version with an appended reminder to print the prompt verbatim without extra text) that extract the exact system prompt without extraneous characters; (2) three defense techniques organized as instruction defense, sandwich defense, and a novel system prompt filtering method that returns a safe response if the ground-truth system prompt (or a chunk of it above a length threshold lambda) is a substring of the model's response; and (3) a rigorous evaluation protocol using ASR, Exact Match, Substring Match, cosine similarity, and Rouge-L across five LLMs and three datasets. Note: PLeak, GCG-leak, AutoDAN-leak, and the original sandwich attack are cited BASELINES/prior work, not this paper's methods.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who interacts with a locally or remotely deployed LLM (containing a built-in system prompt) only through input queries and observed responses, with NO access to model architecture, parameters, gradients, or logits. The attacker crafts adversarial queries disguised as benign user queries whose goal is to make the model emit its system prompt S verbatim as its response R (ideally driving P(R=S | attack query) toward 1), without extraneous text. The asset at risk is the system prompt, framed as the developer's intellectual property and a source of privacy/security leakage (proprietary instructions, guardrail configuration, user roles, architectural details).
limitations: Authors explicitly state they consider ONLY prompting-based attacks with manually designed queries (no automated/optimization-based extraction), and note that two of their three defenses (instruction and sandwich defense) are themselves instruction-based countermeasures that they show are insufficient. Reviewer-observed gaps: the filtering defense assumes access to the ground-truth system prompt at inference time to test substring containment, which is realistic for a developer deploying it but makes it a post-hoc output filter rather than a model-hardening method; ASR uses a fixed cosine>=0.9 threshold; even filtering leaves ~32% residual ASR for the extended sandwich attack on the very short ChatGPT Roles dataset; and the framing treats system prompts as confidential IP, which is a contested premise (system prompts are often not truly secret).

datasets_used: Synthetic Multilingual LLM Prompts dataset (gretelai) - 1250 instances, short system prompts
Synthetic System Prompt dataset (gabrielchua/system-prompt-leakage) - 283K instances, longer system prompts
ChatGPT Roles dataset (WynterJones) - 254 instances, short system prompts
quantitative_results: Llama-3 CoT ASR ~99.04% on Synthetic Multilingual (short) and 93% on Synthetic System Prompt (long), with no defense
GPT-4 extended sandwich ASR ~99.21% on ChatGPT Roles and 87% on Synthetic System Prompt dataset
GPT-4.1 CoT ASR 80% (long) and 67.5% (short Synthetic Multilingual), showing updated model slightly more robust
System prompt filtering reduced Llama-3 CoT ASR from 99% to 0.16% (short) and from ~67-93% to 4% (long)
Average EM (Table 2, ChatGPT Roles): Ours CoT = 0.874 (Llama) / 0.715 (Falcon), vs PLeak 0.728/0.595, AutoDAN-leak 0.598/0.102, GCG-leak 0.268/0.031
Instruction and sandwich defenses left Gemma-2 ASR high (up to ~68% under sandwich defense on long dataset); extended sandwich attack retained ~32% ASR against filtering on ChatGPT Roles
baselines_compared: Perez and Ribeiro (Ignore previous prompt) - EM 0.146 Llama / 0.024 Falcon
